references.title: Time-on-Task Estimation by data-driven Outlier Detection based on Learning Activities

extracted.Courses: One PG and one UG course
extracted.Features: [Log in, Log out, Dashboard viewed, Dashboard reset, Category viewed, Courses Searched, Course viewed, Forum, Calendar Susbcription, Calendar Event updates, Calendar Event Deletes, Calendar Events Created, Badges Viewed, Notifications Viewed, Blog Entries Viewed, User Reports Viewed, Grade Overview, User PW updated, User updated, User Profile viewed, Tag Added/Reemoved, Tag Created/Deleted, Message Sent, Group Message Sent, Message Deleted, Message Viewed, Message Contact Added, User List Viewed, User Profile Viewed, Blog Entries Viewed, User Report Viewed]
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Authors removed multiple irrelevant logs and group the remaining ones according to a taxonomy of Authentication, Overall Site, Profile and Social Interactions.

These are later grouped into components.

From here, the authors look at the behavior of students across different components - aggregate them into learning activities and look at the learning sequence to identify abnormal times on task - outliers.


The authors look at course and activity to identify how to deal wioth these outliers.

Authors compare 6 distinct forms of outlier treatment.
extracted.Comments:
Authors propose a distinct way of identifyng outliers when it comes to time spent on tasks. Authors compare 6 forms of outlier definition and treatment:

MeanSTD: Replace outliers with mean

MedianMAD: uses Median to fill

IQRMed: Uses Median on IQR

IQRWinsor: Winsorizes

Z-Score: Removes all outside regular z-score range

TrimmedMean: Uses a trimmed mean instad of the full mean.

Other than that, authors also consider different possible absolute of thresholds for activities.
extracted.Limitations:
-  No model assessment strategy

 - Small sample size

 - Only uses Multiple Linear Regression

annotations.text: Time-on-task is a popular construct in the learning analytics literature. However, pre-processing details are often unmentioned which affects reproducibility of steps done. Authors propose a method for time on task estimation that takes into account differences in activities. Real-world experiments show that method outperforms current state-of-the-art.
